h1Title: Agalactia: Absent Milk Production After Birth
heroOverview: Agalactia (ICD-10: O92.3) is the complete absence of breast milk secretion following childbirth, distinguished from hypogalactia — insufficient but not absent milk — by the total failure of lactogenesis. True primary agalactia, where no milk is ever produced, affects fewer than 1% of postpartum women, but secondary agalactia — where milk that initially began to come in subsequently ceases entirely — is more common and almost always has an identifiable medical cause. The condition matters clinically because breast milk provides unique immunological, nutritional, and bonding benefits to the infant, and because persistent complete absence of milk after several days postpartum should prompt a search for an underlying hormonal or systemic disorder rather than acceptance of failure.

keyStats.prevalence: True primary agalactia affects fewer than 1% of postpartum women; secondary agalactia rates vary widely depending on definition and study population (WHO/UNICEF breastfeeding data)
keyStats.demographics: Any postpartum woman; higher rates with premature delivery, caesarean section, retained placental fragments, pituitary disorders, and breast surgery
keyStats.avgAge: Follows the age distribution of childbearing, with highest absolute numbers in women aged 20–35
keyStats.globalCases: Globally, only 44% of infants under 6 months are exclusively breastfed (WHO, 2023), with agalactia and hypogalactia among the leading medical contributors to early cessation

definition: Agalactia (ICD-10: O92.3) is the complete cessation or total failure of milk secretion in a postpartum woman. It differs from hypogalactia (O92.4), which denotes inadequate but present milk production. Primary agalactia is the failure of milk to come in at all after delivery; secondary agalactia is the abrupt or progressive cessation of established lactation before weaning is intended. Under normal physiology, the fall in progesterone after placental delivery removes inhibition of prolactin, which triggers lactogenesis II — the onset of copious milk secretion — within 30–72 hours. Any interruption in this prolactin pathway, whether structural (pituitary damage), functional (retained placenta preventing progesterone drop), pharmacological (dopamine agonists), or iatrogenic (early formula supplementation suppressing suckling stimulus), can produce agalactia. The condition falls under obstetrics and lactation medicine, and evaluation involves the same endocrine and anatomical assessments used in disorders of prolactin secretion.

typesClassification.description: Milk never comes in after delivery; often associated with Sheehan syndrome, pituitary aplasia, severe mammary gland underdevelopment, or a prior breast surgery disrupting ductal anatomy.

Established lactation stops abruptly or progressively; causes include retained placental fragments, severe postpartum illness, dopamine-agonist drugs, extreme stress, and prolonged separation from the infant.

Milk supply extinguished by inappropriate use of dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline) to intentionally suppress lactation, or by scheduled formula supplementation that eliminates the suckling stimulus.

primarySymptoms: Complete absence of milk letdown or colostrum secretion within the first 3–5 days postpartum, which is the defining feature.
Breasts that remain soft and non-engorged after delivery, indicating absence of milk accumulation.
Failure of any milk expression despite repeated manual or pump expression over several days.
Infant showing persistent signs of hunger — rooting, crying, and frequent ineffective nursing — that are not satisfied after feeding.
Infant weight loss exceeding 10% of birth weight by day 3–5 without signs of improvement.
Infant producing fewer than 3 wet nappies per day by day 3–4, suggesting inadequate intake.
Absence of the tingling or 'let-down' sensation that normally accompanies feeding or stimulation.
Infant appearing jaundiced early and intensely due to inadequate fluid intake (breastfeeding jaundice).
No galactorrhea or spontaneous milk leakage at any point in the early postpartum period.
Mother reporting that her breasts feel exactly the same as before pregnancy despite days of attempted breastfeeding.
earlyWarningSigns: No colostrum visible on nipple squeezing by day 2–3 after a full-term delivery.
Breasts that did not enlarge or become tender at any point during the third trimester.
History of prior breast surgery involving the areola or major ducts.
Known pituitary disorder or a history of severe postpartum haemorrhage with circulatory collapse (Sheehan syndrome risk).
Infant weight loss above 7% at day 3 with no signs of milk coming in.
emergencySigns: An infant showing signs of dehydration — sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, no urine in more than 8 hours — requires immediate medical evaluation and supplemental feeding.
Infant weight loss above 10% of birth weight demands same-day clinical review and a feeding plan.
Maternal collapse, severe headache, or visual disturbance postpartum in the context of absent milk should raise suspicion for Sheehan syndrome and requires urgent endocrine evaluation.
A breastfeeding newborn with serum sodium above 150 mmol/L (hypernatraemic dehydration) is a neonatal emergency.

causes.description: Even small retained fragments continue secreting progesterone, which suppresses the prolactin-driven onset of copious milk secretion; removal of the retained tissue is often followed by milk coming in within 24–72 hours, confirming this as the mechanism.

Severe postpartum haemorrhage causes infarction of the anterior pituitary during its enlarged postpartum state; prolactin deficiency results in complete failure of lactogenesis, often the first sign that brings the syndrome to attention.

causes.cause: Dopamine agonists and other milk-suppressing drugs
causes.description: Bromocriptine and cabergoline suppress prolactin secretion by stimulating pituitary dopamine receptors; when given therapeutically to suppress lactation after pregnancy loss, they reliably produce complete agalactia if administered promptly.

Augmentation or reduction mammoplasty with periareolar incisions, nipple grafting procedures, and galactocele or abscess drainage can sever the major lactiferous ducts, making milk transit anatomically impossible regardless of secretory capacity.

A subset of women have insufficient lactocyte mass due to primary mammary hypoplasia — often presenting with tubular breast morphology and widely spaced breasts — limiting secretory capacity regardless of hormonal drive.

causes.cause: Severe systemic illness or physiological stress
causes.description: Critical illness, massive haemorrhage, prolonged ICU admission, or severe psychological trauma in the early postpartum period can suppress hypothalamic prolactin-releasing signals through excess cortisol and catecholamine release, shutting down lactogenesis.

causes.cause: Premature birth before full glandular development
causes.description: The breast undergoes significant alveolar development in the third trimester; very preterm delivery before 32 weeks can interrupt this process, reducing secretory tissue available for lactogenesis.

riskFactors.description: Blood loss exceeding 1 litre postpartum risks Sheehan syndrome through anterior pituitary ischaemia; the risk increases proportionally with the severity of haemorrhage and associated hypotension.
Periareolar incisions used in augmentation, reduction, and mastopexy can disrupt the main lactiferous ducts; the more central and larger the incision, the greater the risk of impaired milk flow.
Incomplete placental delivery is identifiable and treatable; removal resolves the progesterone-mediated suppression of lactogenesis in most cases.
Cabergoline and bromocriptine are potent lactation suppressants; accidental exposure or error in prescribing after a live birth can eliminate milk supply within days.

Some data suggest insulin resistance and androgen excess in PCOS impair lactocyte differentiation; breastfeeding difficulties are more common in women with PCOS, though the mechanism is incompletely understood.

Thyroid hormones are required for normal prolactin receptor expression and milk protein synthesis; untreated hypothyroidism diagnosed during pregnancy or postpartum can impair milk production and is readily corrected with levothyroxine.
Preterm birth before 32 weeks interrupts alveolar development and often necessitates maternal-infant separation, reducing the suckling stimulus that maintains prolactin levels.

riskFactors.factor: Early formula supplementation without medical indication
riskFactors.description: Supplementing with formula in the first days postpartum without a clinical reason reduces infant suckling frequency, lowering prolactin pulses and potentially suppressing milk onset — a modifiable driver of lactation failure.

affectedDemographics: Postpartum women of all ages, with higher rates after traumatic deliveries complicated by haemorrhage
Women with pituitary disorders or prior hypophysitis
Women who had periareolar breast surgery
Women with polycystic ovary syndrome or untreated hypothyroidism
Women who delivered very preterm and are expressing milk for an infant in a neonatal unit
Women with tubular or widely spaced breast morphology suggesting insufficient glandular tissue
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing agalactia begins with confirming the absence of milk — no colostrum or milk by manual expression, no infant weight recovery, and no let-down sensation — and then systematically identifying the cause. The history should cover delivery details (haemorrhage, placental delivery completeness), breast anatomy and surgical history, current medications, and thyroid symptoms. Physical examination assesses breast morphology for signs of glandular hypoplasia and breast surgery scarring. The minimum blood workup includes serum prolactin (low or inappropriately normal in Sheehan syndrome and pituitary disorders), thyroid-stimulating hormone (to exclude hypothyroidism), a full blood count, and, if retained placenta is suspected, a uterine ultrasound. In women with postpartum haemorrhage and agalactia, a pituitary MRI is indicated to detect Sheehan syndrome, which is frequently diagnosed late. Lactation consultants play a central assessment role, using test weights before and after feeds and observation of infant latch to distinguish true agalactia from mechanical problems that mimic it.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pituitary prolactin deficiency as a cause; a very low prolactin in a lactating-age postpartum woman strongly suggests Sheehan syndrome or another anterior pituitary failure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood draw; prolactin is highest in the morning and falls throughout the day, so timing matters for interpretation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects retained placental fragments as the cause of secondary agalactia; retained tissue appears as heterogeneous echogenicity in the uterine cavity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A transabdominal or transvaginal ultrasound scan taking 10–15 minutes; transvaginal provides better resolution but is not always necessary.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypothyroidism, which impairs milk protein synthesis and prolactin receptor function; postpartum thyroiditis can emerge weeks after delivery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results return within hours at most hospital laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Sheehan syndrome (pituitary necrosis or empty sella), prolactinoma, or other structural pituitary disease when prolactin levels are abnormal or the history suggests postpartum haemorrhage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–45 minute MRI scan with gadolinium contrast; no radiation but requires lying still in the bore of the scanner.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the actual volume of milk transferred per feed by weighing the infant before and after nursing; defines the severity of agalactia and guides supplementation decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The infant is weighed on a precise digital scale immediately before and after a full feed, without changing clothing or nappies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: When Sheehan syndrome is suspected, assesses the entire anterior pituitary (cortisol, ACTH, LH, FSH, GH, IGF-1) since multiple hormones are often deficient simultaneously.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multiple fasting blood draws, sometimes with stimulation tests (insulin tolerance test) performed under specialist supervision.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of agalactia is cause-specific. When retained placental fragments are identified, surgical or medical evacuation of the uterus — curettage or misoprostol — is the primary treatment and often leads to milk onset within days. When hypothyroidism is found, levothyroxine replacement restores the hormonal environment needed for milk synthesis. Sheehan syndrome requires full pituitary hormone replacement, and the absent prolactin cannot be replaced with a currently approved agent, so the focus shifts to optimising donor milk or formula feeding safely. For women with hypoplastic breast tissue or post-surgical ductal disruption, relactation support with galactagogues (domperidone at 10 mg three times daily or metoclopramide at 10 mg three times daily for short courses) can sometimes augment residual supply, with modest evidence of a 10–50% increase in output in selected women. Frequent stimulation — 8–12 times per day by direct breastfeeding or a hospital-grade double electric pump — is the most evidence-based non-pharmacological intervention. Skin-to-skin (kangaroo mother care) and biological nurturing positions promote oxytocin release, which facilitates any residual let-down. Donor human milk is the recommended supplement when available, as an alternative to formula for infants under 6 months. Lactation consultant support throughout is essential to set realistic expectations, support the maternal-infant relationship, and provide a safe feeding plan when milk supply cannot be established.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of retained placenta
medicalTreatments.description: Surgical curettage or medical management with misoprostol to remove retained placental tissue that is suppressing lactogenesis via persistent progesterone secretion; often definitively resolves secondary agalactia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Milk onset is reported within 24–72 hours in approximately 60–80% of women with retained tissue after removal, based on case series and clinical experience.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine for hypothyroidism
medicalTreatments.description: Thyroid hormone replacement in postpartum hypothyroidism corrects impaired prolactin receptor expression and milk protein synthesis; standard dosing guided by TSH normalisation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalisation of TSH correlates with improved milk supply in hypothyroid women, though controlled trial data specific to agalactia are limited; clinical improvement is typically seen within 4–6 weeks of euthyroidism.

medicalTreatments.name: Domperidone (galactagogue)
medicalTreatments.description: A peripheral dopamine antagonist that raises prolactin levels; used off-label to augment marginal milk supply when residual glandular tissue is present. The standard dose is 10 mg three times daily for 4–8 weeks under medical supervision, with cardiac monitoring given rare QTc prolongation risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show an average increase in milk volume of 30–50% over placebo in women with hypogalactia; less evidence for complete agalactia, where it augments residual supply but cannot initiate absent lactogenesis.

medicalTreatments.name: Frequent breast stimulation
medicalTreatments.description: A hospital-grade double electric pump used 8–12 times per day, beginning as soon as possible after delivery, maximises prolactin pulse frequency and maintains glandular stimulus; the most evidence-based intervention for mothers of preterm infants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pumping initiation within 6 hours of delivery and 8+ times per day is associated with significantly higher milk volumes at 2 weeks compared to delayed or infrequent expression in randomised studies of mothers of preterm infants.

medicalTreatments.name: Pituitary hormone replacement (Sheehan syndrome)
medicalTreatments.description: Women with Sheehan syndrome require cortisol, thyroid hormone, sex steroid, and growth hormone replacement; prolactin deficiency itself has no approved hormonal replacement, so the management goal shifts to ensuring infant nutrition via donor milk or formula.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Correct replacement of other pituitary hormones significantly improves maternal wellbeing and long-term health outcomes; lactation cannot be restored in prolactin-deficient Sheehan syndrome.

alternativeTreatments.name: Galactagogue herbs (fenugreek, moringa)
alternativeTreatments.description: Fenugreek seed and moringa leaf are the most studied herbal galactagogues; small trials show modest increases in expressed milk volume, but evidence is insufficient to recommend them as primary treatment and they do not address the underlying cause of agalactia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Some small trials suggest acupuncture may improve milk production through oxytocin pathways, but the evidence is too limited and heterogeneous to support routine use as a primary treatment for agalactia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Skin-to-skin contact and biological nurturing
alternativeTreatments.description: Prolonged skin-to-skin contact promotes oxytocin release, supports any residual let-down reflex, and improves the infant's feeding cues; recommended as a first-line supportive measure regardless of the primary treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Agalactia beyond 5 days postpartum without an identified cause warrants assessment by an obstetrician, lactation medicine specialist, or endocrinologist. Retained placenta requires prompt surgical management. Sheehan syndrome carries long-term health implications beyond lactation — untreated adrenal insufficiency in this condition is life-threatening — making specialist diagnosis essential. Women with pituitary disorders should be managed by an endocrinologist throughout the reproductive period.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an obstetrician or obstetric physician with experience in postpartum endocrine disorders, or a physician trained in lactation medicine (IBCLC-accredited specialist). For suspected pituitary disease, an endocrinologist with a reproductive medicine interest is needed. An International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC) is the appropriate allied health professional for ongoing breastfeeding support and feeding plan design.
preventionStrategies: Ensure complete placental delivery is confirmed immediately after birth; any retained fragments should be managed before discharge.
Establish breastfeeding within 30–60 minutes of delivery and feed at least 8–12 times per day in the first week to maximise prolactin stimulation.
Avoid routine formula supplementation without medical indication in the first days, which can reduce the suckling stimulus.
Screen for and treat hypothyroidism in pregnant women, as postpartum thyroid dysfunction impairs lactation.
For mothers delivering preterm or by caesarean, arrange lactation consultant support and hospital-grade pump access from day 1.
Inform lactation consultants and midwives of any history of breast surgery before delivery so extra support can be anticipated.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain adequate fluid and caloric intake during lactation — under-eating or dehydration worsens milk supply.
Minimise extreme physical or psychological stress in the early postpartum period, as cortisol and catecholamines suppress oxytocin and prolactin.
Sleep in short cycles synchronised with the infant's feeding schedule to maintain prolactin pulsatility, rather than extended separation at night.
Avoid pseudoephedrine, combined oral contraceptives, and large amounts of sage or peppermint, all of which can reduce milk supply.
Keep detailed records of feeding frequency, infant wet nappies, and expressed milk volumes to provide clinicians with objective data.

recommended: At least 1,800–2,200 kcal per day during lactation to support milk synthesis
Adequate protein from varied sources including legumes, dairy, eggs, and lean meats
Oats, which are a traditional galactagogue with some supportive pilot data
Generous water intake — at least 2–3 litres per day — as milk production requires additional fluid
avoid: Extreme caloric restriction or crash dieting during lactation attempts
Large quantities of sage, spearmint, and peppermint, which are anecdotally reported to reduce milk supply
Alcohol, which blunts the oxytocin let-down reflex and passes into breast milk
Highly caffeinated products in excess, though moderate coffee intake does not significantly affect supply

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise does not reduce milk supply and is encouraged for postpartum health and mood. Strenuous exercise without adequate caloric intake can temporarily reduce milk production by diverting energy. If milk supply is marginal, breastfeed or pump before exercise and ensure full post-exercise rehydration and nutrition.
dailyManagement: Record pumping or nursing sessions, volumes expressed, and infant wet and dirty nappy counts in a feeding diary to track progress.
Attend scheduled lactation consultant appointments — frequency matters most in the first 2 weeks when supply is being established.
Take galactagogue medications exactly as prescribed and do not stop abruptly without medical advice.
If infant supplementation is necessary, use a supplemental nursing system (at-breast supplementer) where possible to maintain breastfeeding stimulus while ensuring adequate intake.
Seek emotional support — feeding difficulties carry significant psychological weight; post-natal support groups and mental health resources should be accessed early.
prognosis: The outlook for agalactia depends almost entirely on the underlying cause. Women with retained placenta who undergo timely evacuation have a good probability of establishing normal lactation within 2–3 days. Women with hypothyroidism who achieve euthyroidism can expect improved supply within weeks. Women with Sheehan syndrome or severe glandular hypoplasia will not be able to breastfeed fully and should be counselled supportively and provided with a safe infant formula feeding plan. In the absence of a structural or endocrine cause, most women with secondary agalactia respond to a combination of galactagogue therapy, frequent pumping, and lactation support, though the response is variable. Early intervention within the first 2 weeks improves outcomes significantly, as breast glandular tissue atrophies rapidly with disuse.
recoveryTimeline: With effective treatment of the underlying cause, milk onset or return typically occurs within 24–72 hours for retained placenta and 4–8 weeks for hypothyroidism. Galactagogue effects are usually apparent within 1–2 weeks of initiation. Relactation after complete cessation beyond 4 weeks is achievable but requires 2–6 weeks of sustained effort with frequent pumping. Any resumption of milk should be celebrated as meaningful supplementary nutrition even if exclusive breastfeeding cannot be achieved.
complications: Infant hypernatraemic dehydration — a serious neonatal complication of unrecognised agalactia; serum sodium above 150 mmol/L requires hospital admission and careful rehydration.
Neonatal jaundice — insufficient intake impairs bilirubin excretion; breastfeeding jaundice can require phototherapy.
Maternal psychological distress — inability to breastfeed carries significant rates of guilt, grief, and postnatal depression, warranting proactive psychological support.
Missed diagnosis of Sheehan syndrome — permanent panhypopituitarism, adrenal insufficiency, and osteoporosis if the underlying cause remains undiagnosed.
Premature weaning — without support, many women facing agalactia discontinue breastfeeding attempts before a potentially reversible cause has been identified and treated.

supportResources.name: La Leche League International — Breastfeeding Help
supportResources.description: Peer support network and information resources for breastfeeding challenges including low supply and agalactia.

supportResources.name: WHO — Breastfeeding recommendations and support
supportResources.description: World Health Organization guidance on breastfeeding, donor milk, and infant nutrition for families and health workers.

supportResources.name: ACOG — Breastfeeding Challenges (Patient FAQ)
supportResources.description: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists patient FAQ on breastfeeding difficulties, including low milk supply.

sources.title: ABM Clinical Protocol #9: Use of Galactagogues in Initiating or Augmenting Milk Production, 2nd revision 2018
Stuebe AM, Schwarz EB. The risks and benefits of infant feeding practices for women and their children. J Perinatol, 2010
Zargar AH et al. Sheehan's syndrome: still a common cause of hypopituitarism in developing countries. Ann Saudi Med, 2005
Grzeskowiak LE et al. Domperidone for increasing breast milk volume in mothers expressing breast milk for their preterm infants: a systematic review and meta-analysis. BJOG, 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypogalactia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypogalactia is insufficient but present milk production; agalactia is complete absence. The distinction matters because hypogalactia often responds to galactagogues and frequent stimulation, whereas agalactia frequently has a structural or hormonal cause needing specific treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Suppressed lactation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Suppressed lactation is the intentional or drug-mediated stopping of milk production; agalactia is the failure of production to begin or continue despite the mother's wish to breastfeed. Management goals are opposite.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mastitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mastitis causes breast pain, swelling, and fever from infection or inflammation; milk is usually still produced but may transiently decrease. Agalactia involves complete absence of milk without necessarily having breast pain or infection.

relatedConditions.name: Hypogalactia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypogalactia and agalactia share causes and management approaches; they form a clinical spectrum from markedly reduced to absent milk production.

relatedConditions.name: Suppressed lactation
relatedConditions.relevance: When suppression agents are given in error or the clinical picture is unclear, suppressed lactation may be confused with pathological agalactia.

relatedConditions.name: Mastitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe mastitis or breast abscess can transiently suppress milk production and, if inadequately treated, may progress to secondary agalactia.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid hormone is required for normal prolactin receptor expression; hypothyroidism is one of the most common and treatable causes of agalactia and hypogalactia.

faqs.question: What is agalactia?
faqs.answer: Agalactia is the complete absence of breast milk after childbirth. It differs from low milk supply (hypogalactia) in that no milk is produced at all. It is usually caused by a medical reason such as retained placenta, pituitary damage, or hormonal imbalance, and should prompt investigation.

faqs.question: What causes no milk to come in after birth?
faqs.answer: The most common medical causes are retained placental fragments (which keep progesterone high and block milk onset), underactive thyroid, pituitary gland damage after severe postpartum bleeding (Sheehan syndrome), and prior breast surgery that cut the milk ducts. Insufficient breast glandular tissue from birth is a less common cause.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have agalactia or just low milk supply?
faqs.answer: Agalactia means no milk at all — no colostrum visible by day 2–3, no let-down sensation, and no measurable milk on pumping. Low milk supply means some milk is present but less than the baby needs. A lactation consultant can assess with test weights before and after feeding to measure exactly how much milk is transferred.

faqs.question: Can agalactia be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes, in many cases. If retained placental tissue is found, removing it often causes milk to come in within days. Treating an underactive thyroid with levothyroxine usually improves supply within weeks. Galactagogue medications and frequent pumping can help when some glandular tissue is present. Pituitary damage causing complete prolactin loss cannot currently be treated with hormonal replacement.

faqs.question: Is agalactia permanent?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Agalactia from retained placenta or hypothyroidism is usually reversible with treatment. Agalactia from Sheehan syndrome (pituitary damage) or severe breast surgery is likely to be permanent. Most women with secondary agalactia who receive prompt support and treatment can re-establish at least partial milk production.

faqs.question: What is Sheehan syndrome and how does it cause agalactia?
faqs.answer: Sheehan syndrome occurs when severe bleeding during childbirth damages the pituitary gland. The pituitary enlarges during pregnancy, making it vulnerable to ischaemia if blood pressure drops severely. Damage destroys prolactin-producing cells, so milk never comes in. Women with Sheehan syndrome also lose other pituitary hormones and need lifelong hormone replacement.

faqs.question: Can I still bond with my baby if I cannot breastfeed?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Feeding — whether breast or bottle — is a powerful bonding opportunity. Skin-to-skin contact, responsiveness to your baby's cues, and attentive feeding regardless of the method build secure attachment. Many parents who cannot breastfeed develop strong, nurturing bonds through bottle feeding, cuddle time, and consistent care.

faqs.question: Will my baby be harmed by not getting breast milk?
faqs.answer: Breast milk provides unique immune and nutritional benefits, but modern infant formula is a safe alternative when breast milk is not available. The immediate clinical priority is ensuring the baby receives adequate calories and fluids. Donor breast milk from a regulated human milk bank is recommended for very preterm or sick infants when maternal milk is unavailable.

faqs.question: What is a galactagogue?
faqs.answer: A galactagogue is any drug, food, or herb used to promote or increase breast milk production. The most evidence-based pharmaceutical galactagogues are domperidone and metoclopramide. Herbal options include fenugreek and moringa. Galactagogues are most effective when combined with frequent breast stimulation and are unlikely to help if a structural or hormonal barrier to lactation is not first addressed.

faqs.question: Can pumping help if I have no milk?
faqs.answer: Pumping stimulates prolactin pulses and can help initiate or maintain milk supply when some glandular tissue is present. However, if the cause is structural (damaged ducts, insufficient tissue) or hormonal (absent prolactin), pumping alone will not overcome the barrier. Pumping 8–12 times per day is the gold standard supportive measure while the underlying cause is investigated.

faqs.question: Does stress cause agalactia?
faqs.answer: Severe acute stress — such as a traumatic delivery, postpartum haemorrhage, or ICU admission — can suppress prolactin and oxytocin through cortisol and catecholamine release, causing secondary agalactia. Everyday parenting stress rarely causes complete milk failure. Skin-to-skin contact and a calm feeding environment support the oxytocin-mediated let-down reflex.

faqs.question: Can I relactate if my milk stopped?
faqs.answer: Relactation — re-establishing milk production after it has stopped — is possible within the first several weeks if glandular tissue is intact. It requires sustained, frequent breast stimulation (8+ times daily), ideally combined with galactagogue medication, and takes 2–6 weeks of consistent effort. The younger the infant and the shorter the gap since lactation ceased, the higher the likelihood of success.

faqs.question: What is donor breast milk?
faqs.answer: Donor breast milk is milk from screened, healthy lactating women, pasteurised and provided through a regulated human milk bank. It is recommended for preterm or medically vulnerable infants when maternal milk is unavailable. Pasteurisation destroys pathogens but preserves most immunological components. Ask your neonatal unit or lactation consultant about local milk banking services.

faqs.question: Should I take domperidone for agalactia?
faqs.answer: Domperidone raises prolactin levels and can increase milk output by 30–50% in women with some residual supply. It is not appropriate if the cause is structural gland damage or complete pituitary failure. It requires a prescription, carries a small risk of cardiac arrhythmia (QTc prolongation) requiring monitoring, and should only be used under medical supervision for a defined course.

faqs.question: Can polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) cause agalactia?
faqs.answer: PCOS is associated with breastfeeding difficulties in some women, possibly through insulin resistance impairing lactocyte differentiation or androgen excess affecting glandular development. The link is not established strongly enough to blame PCOS alone for complete agalactia. Women with PCOS should be offered enhanced lactation support proactively.

faqs.question: How is retained placenta diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Retained placental fragments are diagnosed by pelvic ultrasound, which shows echogenic tissue within the uterine cavity. Levels of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) that remain elevated postpartum also suggest retained trophoblastic tissue. Blood tests alone are not sufficient; imaging is required for definitive diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is agalactia hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is no clear hereditary pattern for most agalactia. Insufficient glandular tissue may run in families, and some women report that their mother also had breastfeeding difficulties, suggesting a genetic contribution to mammary development in rare cases. Hormonal causes such as thyroid disease and PCOS have genetic components but are not transmitted as simple Mendelian traits.

faqs.question: What emotional support is available for agalactia?
faqs.answer: La Leche League offers peer support groups and one-to-one help from trained breastfeeding counsellors. Hospital lactation consultants provide clinical guidance. Postnatal mental health services should be accessed proactively, as feeding failure is closely associated with postnatal depression. Online communities of parents who have faced breastfeeding challenges can also reduce isolation.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about absent milk?
faqs.answer: See a clinician if you have no colostrum or milk by day 3–4 after a full-term delivery, if your baby is losing more than 10% of birth weight, or if your baby is producing fewer than 3 wet nappies per day. Seek urgent care if your baby appears dehydrated or lethargic. Do not wait beyond 5 days postpartum to seek medical advice for absent milk.

metaDescription: Agalactia is the complete absence of breast milk after birth. Learn medical causes, from retained placenta to pituitary damage, and evidence-based treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
